articles.headline: EAM Jaishankar Engages EU Counterparts on West Asia, Ukraine, Trade in Brussels
articles.explainer:
• External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar visited Brussels for discussions with European Union officials.
• He met with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and was invited to the EU Foreign Affairs Council meeting on March 16, 2026.
• Key topics included the situation in West Asia and Ukraine, with shared objectives of de-escalation, stability, and energy security.
• Discussions also covered trade, investment, technology, mobility, and defence, aiming for early implementation of the EU-India trade deal signed in January.
• The visit underscores India's active engagement with multilateral blocs on critical global and bilateral issues.

The External Affairs Minister's engagement with the European Union leadership and foreign ministers highlights India's proactive diplomacy in navigating complex global challenges and strengthening strategic partnerships. Discussions on West Asia and Ukraine underscore India's commitment to global peace and stability, while also addressing its energy security concerns. The focus on implementing the trade deal reflects India's economic diplomacy, aiming to enhance trade, investment, and technological cooperation, crucial for achieving inclusive growth and sustainable development. This interaction reinforces India's role as a responsible global actor, contributing to a multipolar world order and fostering cooperative federalism in international relations.

articles.headline: National Quantum Mission Advances: 23 Institutions Approved for Quantum Labs
articles.explainer:
• Twenty-three academic institutions across India have received approval to establish quantum teaching laboratories.
• This initiative falls under the ambit of the National Quantum Mission (NQM), a flagship government program.
• The NQM has an approved budget of ₹6003.65 crore for the period 2023–2031.
• Its ambitious goals include developing 50–1,000 qubit quantum computers and satellite-based secure communication.
• The mission also aims to advance high-precision quantum sensors and materials, indicating a comprehensive approach to quantum technology.

The approval of quantum teaching laboratories under the National Quantum Mission marks a significant stride in India's pursuit of cutting-edge scientific and technological advancements. This strategic investment in quantum computing, communication, and sensing is crucial for national security, economic competitiveness, and future innovation. By fostering research and development in academic institutions, the government aims to build a robust ecosystem for quantum technologies, aligning with the vision of Atmanirbhar Bharat. This initiative will contribute to human resource development in specialized fields, address the digital divide by enabling secure communication, and potentially revolutionize sectors from healthcare to defense, thereby promoting sustainable development and technological sovereignty.

articles.headline: Geopolitical Risks Drive Oil Price Volatility, Impacting Global Energy Security
articles.explainer:
• Crude oil prices surged to $118 a barrel last week, reflecting heightened geopolitical tensions, particularly in West Asia.
• Prices have more than doubled since mid-December 2025, when Brent crude was at $57.56, and remain in three digits.
• The ongoing conflict involving Israel and Iran has created tangible barriers to supply, effectively sidelining 20% of global supplies.
• Vulnerability of global maritime arteries like the Strait of Hormuz (one-fifth of global oil consumption) and Bab el-Mandeb/Suez corridor (one-tenth of seaborne crude) is stark.
• Geopolitical events now directly influence freight rates, shipping insurance, and vessel availability, transforming oil into a political instrument.

The analysis highlights a critical shift in global energy markets, where geopolitical risks now exert a profound and sustained influence on crude oil prices beyond traditional supply-demand dynamics. For major importing economies like India, this volatility poses significant challenges to energy security, fiscal stability, and economic development. The vulnerability of strategic maritime chokepoints underscores the imperative for diversified energy sources, robust trade strategies, and proactive diplomatic engagement to mitigate supply chain disruptions. This situation necessitates a comprehensive approach to foreign policy and economic planning, emphasizing strategic autonomy and resilience against external shocks, while also impacting inflation and the balance of payments, crucial for macroeconomic stability.

articles.headline: Western Himalayas Experience Driest Spring in Four Centuries, Study Reveals
articles.explainer:
• Researchers reconstructed 396 years of spring rainfall history in the Western Himalayas, specifically Lahaul, Himachal Pradesh, using tree cores from old deodar trees.
• The study, published by scientists from Birbal Sahni Institute of Palaeosciences (BSIP) and other institutions, identified the spring of 2022 as the driest on record.
• The last two decades (2002-2022) have been identified as the driest 20-year period in the entire 396-year reconstruction.
• Dendroclimatology, the science of determining past climates from tree rings, was employed, where tree ring width indicates past water availability.
• The findings have significant implications for water security, disaster management, and agriculture in northern India, which heavily relies on Himalayan snowmelt.
• Permission from the Forest Department was obtained to extract tree core samples using an increment borer, ensuring minimal damage to the trees.

This research underscores the critical impact of climate change on India's ecological balance and water resources, particularly in the sensitive Himalayan region. The unprecedented dryness in the Western Himalayas, as revealed by dendroclimatology, highlights a severe environmental challenge with far-reaching socio-economic consequences. Diminished snowmelt directly threatens the water security of downstream regions, impacting agricultural productivity and exacerbating the vulnerability to natural disasters like droughts and flash floods. This situation necessitates robust climate adaptation strategies, integrated water resource management, and enhanced disaster preparedness. The findings reinforce the urgency of national climate action plans and international cooperation to mitigate global warming, aligning with India's commitments under the Paris Agreement and Sustainable Development Goals related to climate resilience and water management. Understanding these long-term climate shifts is crucial for informed policy-making in environmental governance and sustainable development.

articles.headline: Transgender Communities Nationwide Protest Bill Redefining Gender Identity
articles.explainer:
• Transgender communities and civil society groups across India are protesting a Union government's proposed legislation.
• The Bill aims to withdraw the right to "self-perceived gender identity" and redefine who constitutes a "transgender person."
• Protests and public meetings have occurred in major cities including New Delhi, Pune, Hyderabad, and Kolkata.
• Coalitions of lawyers, doctors, activists, and professionals are planning campaigns, including writing to MPs and organizing awareness meetings.
• An online public petition demanding the Bill's withdrawal has garnered over 13,000 signatures.
• The protests call for the government to protect the right to self-perceived gender identity, a right previously affirmed by the Supreme Court.

The nationwide protests against the proposed legislation to redefine transgender identity highlight a significant challenge to fundamental rights and social justice in India. The right to self-perceived gender identity is a cornerstone of individual dignity and autonomy, affirmed by the Supreme Court in landmark judgments like NALSA v. Union of India, which recognized transgender persons as the 'third gender' and upheld their rights under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution. Any legislative attempt to restrict this right could be seen as regressive, undermining the principles of equality and non-discrimination. This issue also touches upon the role of civil society in democratic governance, as various groups mobilize to advocate for marginalized communities. Ensuring inclusive growth and protecting the rights of all citizens, including transgender persons, is vital for upholding constitutionalism and fostering a truly equitable society.

articles.headline: Israel Seeks Diplomatic Resolution with Iran Amidst West Asian Conflict
articles.explainer:
• Israel's Ambassador to India, Reuven Azar, stated that Israel is not seeking a "perpetual war" with Iran and is open to diplomatic solutions if Iran "changes course."
• The Ambassador's comments came as the West Asia conflict entered its third week, following joint U.S.-Israel strikes on February 28.
• India's External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar confirmed that his talks with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi yielded results for the safe passage of two LNG-laden ships through the Hormuz Strait.
• Jaishankar emphasized India's preference for reasoning and coordination to find solutions in the region, suggesting a similar approach for global stability.
• Israel claimed to support diplomacy and was in contact with regional countries, proposing that a diplomatic way out could follow the completion of military targets.
• The Iranian government has reportedly rejected current talks or a ceasefire, accusing the U.S. of disrupting dialogue through its attacks.

The ongoing West Asian conflict and the diplomatic overtures from Israel, alongside India's active engagement, underscore the complex dynamics of international relations and regional security. India's emphasis on dialogue and its successful mediation for maritime passage through the Strait of Hormuz highlight its growing role as a responsible global actor and a proponent of multilateralism. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical chokepoint for global energy trade, and any disruption has significant implications for energy security and global supply chains, directly affecting India's economic interests. This situation necessitates careful diplomatic maneuvering to de-escalate tensions and protect international maritime law. India's independent foreign policy, balancing relations with all parties, is crucial for maintaining regional stability and safeguarding its strategic autonomy amidst geopolitical shifts.

articles.headline: India's West Asia Diplomacy Tested Amidst Regional Conflict and Economic Stakes
articles.explainer:
• The ongoing West Asia conflict, which began on February 28, has extended its impact to South Asia, affecting daily necessities, trade, and the safety of citizens.
• Approximately 25 million South Asians, including 10 million Indians, reside and work in West Asia, making their safety a critical concern.
• Indian seafarers constitute about 15% of the global merchant ship crew, facing heightened risks, particularly near the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint.
• India's initial diplomatic posture regarding the conflict, specifically its delayed condolence for Iran's Supreme Leader, contrasted with immediate responses from neighbouring South Asian nations like Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Maldives.
• The conflict has disrupted supply chains for food, fuel, and fertilizers, impacting economic stability across the South Asian region.

This article highlights the complex challenges confronting India's foreign policy and neighbourhood diplomacy in the volatile West Asian region. The conflict's spillover effects, particularly on the large Indian diaspora and maritime trade routes like the Strait of Hormuz, underscore the intricate linkages between regional stability and India's economic interests and national security. India's strategic autonomy and its ability to maintain balanced relations with all stakeholders, including the U.S., Israel, and Iran, are being rigorously tested. The differing diplomatic responses among South Asian nations also reveal the nuances of regional cooperation and the potential for divergent foreign policy approaches. This situation necessitates a robust diplomatic framework to safeguard national interests, ensure the welfare of overseas citizens, and contribute to regional de-escalation, aligning with India's broader vision of a multipolar world order and responsible global citizenship.

articles.headline: China-US Engagements Continue Amidst Sanctions Discussions
articles.explainer:
• China has confirmed ongoing contact with the United States regarding a potential summit between the two nations.
• Beijing has also indicated that sanctions imposed by U.S. Senator Marco Rubio may not apply, suggesting a nuanced diplomatic approach.
• These developments occur amidst complex bilateral relations, marked by trade disputes, technological competition, and geopolitical rivalries.
• High-level dialogues between the U.S. and China are critical for global stability and addressing shared challenges like climate change and economic recovery.
• The potential for a summit and the discussion around sanctions reflect efforts to manage tensions and find areas of cooperation between the two global powers.

The continued engagement between China and the United States, despite prevailing geopolitical tensions and the shadow of sanctions, underscores the intricate nature of great power competition and cooperation. For India, the dynamics of US-China relations hold significant implications for its strategic autonomy, economic partnerships, and regional security architecture. A stable yet competitive relationship between these two powers can create opportunities for India to advance its own interests, while heightened friction could necessitate careful diplomatic balancing. This ongoing dialogue highlights the imperative for multilateralism and a rules-based international order, as the policies of these major economies inevitably shape global trade, technology governance, and the broader international relations landscape, influencing India's foreign policy calculus and economic development trajectory.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Calcutta High Court's NIA Probe Order in Beldanga Violence
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court declined to intervene in a Calcutta High Court decision that refused to stay a National Investigation Agency (NIA) probe into violence in Beldanga, Murshidabad district, West Bengal.
• The Calcutta High Court had confirmed a trial court order to hand over the case diary to the NIA and directed the agency to file a status report by March 24.
• West Bengal challenged the High Court's direction, arguing that the Supreme Court had previously questioned the invocation of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (UAPA) in the case.
• On February 11, the apex court had expressed doubts whether an "emotional outburst" could be termed a "terrorist act" under UAPA without material evidence.
• The Supreme Court, however, found the High Court's confirmation of the NIA probe "quite balanced," stating it was for the High Court to impartially decide on the necessity of an NIA investigation.

This Supreme Court ruling reinforces the principles of judicial review and federalism, particularly concerning the jurisdiction of central investigative agencies and the application of anti-terror legislation. The apex court's decision to uphold the Calcutta High Court's order, despite its earlier reservations on the UAPA's invocation, highlights the judiciary's role in ensuring a balanced approach to law enforcement and constitutional governance. It underscores the delicate balance between state autonomy in maintaining law and order and the central government's prerogative in matters of national security, especially when scheduled offences under acts like UAPA are involved. This case is crucial for understanding the operational dynamics between the High Courts and the Supreme Court, the scope of the NIA Act, and the interpretation of "terrorist act" under UAPA, which has significant implications for fundamental rights and criminal justice administration.

articles.headline: Supreme Court expands Right to Die with Dignity under Article 21, approves withdrawal of life support
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark decision, approved the withdrawal of life support, including artificial nutrition and hydration, for Harish Rana, who was in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for over 12 years.
• The Court invoked the constitutional idea of dignity, stating it's incompatible to keep terminally ill patients in PVS alive solely through technological advancements.
• This judgment expands the right to live with dignity under Article 21 of the Constitution to encompass the right to die with dignity.
• The ruling builds upon previous cases like Smt. Gian Kaur vs The State Of Punjab (1996) and the Aruna Shanbaug petition (2011), where guidelines for withdrawing medical treatment were laid down.
• A 2018 Supreme Court Constitution Bench had already held that the right to die with dignity is a fundamental right.
• The Court also endorsed the concept of a 'living will', a legal document allowing individuals to state preferences for medical treatment if incapacitated.

This Supreme Court judgment significantly reinforces the constitutional right to life and personal liberty under Article 21, interpreting it to include the right to die with dignity. By allowing the withdrawal of life support for individuals in a persistent vegetative state, the judiciary has provided a compassionate framework for end-of-life care, addressing the ethical dilemmas posed by advanced medical technology. This ruling reflects a progressive stance on individual autonomy and human dignity, ensuring that prolonged suffering without hope of recovery does not diminish a person's fundamental rights. The endorsement of 'living wills' empowers citizens to make informed decisions about their medical treatment, promoting patient-centric healthcare and reducing the burden on families. This judicial pronouncement, in the absence of specific legislation, underscores the Supreme Court's role as a guardian of fundamental rights and its capacity to evolve jurisprudence in response to societal and technological changes, impacting public policy on healthcare and individual liberties.

articles.headline: NavIC Satellite Faces Setback with Atomic Clock Failure, Impacts India's Navigation Autonomy
articles.explainer:
PART 1 — KEY FACTS:
• India's indigenous navigation satellite system, NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation), has reportedly experienced a failure in one of its onboard atomic clocks.
• Atomic clocks are crucial for precise timing and accurate positional data, which are fundamental to the functioning of satellite navigation systems.
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) operates NavIC, which currently comprises a constellation of seven satellites.
• This incident could potentially affect the accuracy and reliability of the specific satellite's signals, impacting the overall system's performance.
• NavIC aims to provide accurate real-time positioning and timing services over India and a region extending up to 1,500 km around its boundary.
• The system is designed to provide both Standard Positioning Service (SPS) for civilian users and Restricted Service (RS) for authorised users, including the military.

PART 2 — ANALYSIS: The reported failure of an atomic clock on a NavIC satellite underscores the technical complexities inherent in developing and maintaining advanced space infrastructure. This incident has significant implications for India's strategic autonomy, as NavIC is envisioned to reduce reliance on foreign global navigation satellite systems like GPS. The precision offered by atomic clocks is vital for applications ranging from disaster management and fleet tracking to critical infrastructure and defence. Ensuring the resilience and redundancy of such critical components is paramount for national security and economic development. This event highlights the ongoing challenges in indigenous technology development and the need for robust quality control and backup mechanisms to sustain critical space-based assets, aligning with India's broader vision for self-reliance in space technology.

Science & Technology: Achievements of Indians in S&T

articles.headline: Supreme Court Emphasizes Stakeholder Consultation for Legal Education Reforms
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India expressed reluctance to alter the duration of five-year integrated LL.B. programmes across the country.
• A Bench comprising Chief Justice of India Surya Kant and Justice Joymalya Bagchi stated that policy matters like course duration do not warrant judicial intervention.
• The Court acknowledged the necessity of reforms to strengthen the quality of legal education in India.
• It observed that decisions concerning the structure and duration of professional courses require broader consultation.
• Key stakeholders for such consultations include academic institutions, regulatory bodies (like the Bar Council of India), and other relevant parties.

The Supreme Court's stance on the duration of legal education programs highlights the principle of separation of powers, emphasizing that policy formulation primarily rests with the executive and legislative branches, guided by expert bodies. While the judiciary often intervenes to uphold constitutional rights or ensure good governance, it generally refrains from encroaching upon areas requiring specialized academic and administrative expertise. The Court's call for extensive stakeholder consultation underscores the importance of inclusive decision-making in reforming professional education, ensuring that changes are well-considered, practical, and reflect the diverse needs of students, practitioners, and the legal system. This approach promotes cooperative federalism in education policy and strengthens institutional autonomy, crucial for maintaining the quality and relevance of legal professionals in a dynamic society.

articles.headline: Gujarat Court Convicts Five in 2016 Una Dalit Flogging Case
articles.explainer:
• A court in Veraval, Gujarat, convicted five persons on March 16, 2026, in the 2016 Una Dalit flogging case.
• The incident on July 11, 2016, involved an attack by over 40 men from the Darbar community on four members of the Sarvaiya family in Mota Samadhiyala village near Una.
• The convicted individuals are Ramesh Jadav, Rakesh Joshi, Pramodgiri Gausvami, Nagji Vaniya, and Balwantgiri Gausvami.
• They were found guilty under IPC Sections 323, 324, 342, and 504, pertaining to causing hurt, wrongful confinement, and intentional insult.
• Crucially, convictions were also made under Sections 3(1)(D) and 3(1)(E)(R)(S)(U) of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act.
• 35 other accused were acquitted, and proceedings against one police constable ended due to his death during the trial.

The conviction in the 2016 Una Dalit flogging case, nearly a decade after the incident, underscores the persistent challenges of caste-based discrimination and violence in India. This judgment, particularly the application of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, reinforces the state's commitment to upholding social justice and protecting vulnerable communities. While the legal process has been protracted, the verdict sends a crucial message about accountability and the rule of law in addressing atrocities. It highlights the need for effective implementation of protective legislation, strengthening the criminal justice system, and fostering a societal environment free from caste prejudice. The case also brings to the fore the broader discourse on fundamental rights, equality, and the constitutional mandate to eradicate untouchability, emphasizing the ongoing struggle for dignity and human rights for marginalized sections of society.

articles.headline: MEA Rejects USCIRF Report, Cites Distorted India Picture and Temple Vandalism in U.S.
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) criticized the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF) for its latest report on India.
• MEA spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal stated that USCIRF presents a "distorted and selective picture of India," relying on "questionable sources and ideological narratives."
• The MEA urged USCIRF to highlight incidents of vandalism targeting Hindu temples in the United States instead of criticizing India.
• The USCIRF report had called for "targeted sanctions" against India's Research & Analysis Wing (R&AW) and the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS).
• These sanctions proposed freezing assets and barring entry into the U.S. for individuals and entities deemed responsible for religious freedom violations.

The MEA's strong rebuttal to the USCIRF report underscores India's firm stance against external interference in its internal affairs and its commitment to protecting its national sovereignty. The criticism of the report's methodology highlights concerns about objective reporting and the potential for biased narratives to influence international perceptions. This diplomatic exchange reflects the complexities of bilateral relations, particularly when issues of human rights and religious freedom are raised by international bodies. India's counter-narrative, by pointing to incidents affecting its diaspora abroad, emphasizes the principle of reciprocity and the need for a balanced perspective in assessing religious freedom globally, aligning with its foreign policy objectives of non-interference and mutual respect.

articles.headline: Indian Navy Deploys Warships to Escort Merchant Vessels in Gulf Region
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Navy has deployed warships to escort Indian-flagged merchant vessels transiting through the Persian Gulf region.
• Three Indian Navy ships (Shivalik, Nanda Devi, and Jag Laadki) escorted merchant ships from the Gulf of Oman after they crossed the Strait of Hormuz.
• This deployment ensures the safe transit of Indian vessels through vulnerable international waters.
• The warships are operating under "Operation Sankalp," a mission specifically aimed at safeguarding merchant shipping and protecting India’s maritime interests.
• The mission focuses on the Gulf region, which is crucial for India's trade and energy security.
• The deployment comes amidst heightened regional tensions, emphasizing the need for robust maritime security.

The Indian Navy's deployment of warships to escort merchant vessels in the Gulf of Oman and through the Strait of Hormuz underscores India's commitment to protecting its maritime interests and ensuring the security of global trade routes. "Operation Sankalp" exemplifies India's growing blue-water capabilities and its role as a net security provider in the Indian Ocean Region. This proactive measure is vital for safeguarding India's energy security, as a significant portion of its oil and gas imports transits through these waters. It also reinforces adherence to the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) regarding freedom of navigation. Such deployments are crucial for maintaining regional stability, deterring piracy and other maritime threats, and projecting India's strategic autonomy and influence in a geopolitically sensitive area.

articles.headline: Analysis of Subdued Gold Prices in India Amidst West Asian Conflict
articles.explainer:
• Despite the ongoing West Asian conflict, which typically drives up safe-haven asset demand, gold prices in India have remained subdued.
• This phenomenon contrasts with global trends where geopolitical instability often correlates with rising gold valuations.
• Factors influencing domestic gold prices include local supply-demand dynamics, import duties, exchange rate fluctuations, and investor sentiment.
• The Indian market's unique characteristics, such as seasonal demand patterns and cultural significance of gold, play a role in its pricing.
• The Reserve Bank of India's monetary policy decisions and government's fiscal measures can also indirectly impact gold's attractiveness as an investment.

The divergence of India's gold market from global trends amidst geopolitical tensions in West Asia presents an interesting case study in economic resilience and domestic market dynamics. While gold traditionally serves as a hedge against inflation and uncertainty, its subdued performance in India suggests the overriding influence of internal economic factors. This scenario highlights the complex interplay of fiscal policy, monetary policy, and consumer behaviour in shaping commodity markets. Understanding these dynamics is crucial for macroeconomic stability, as gold imports significantly impact India's current account deficit. Furthermore, it underscores the need for a nuanced approach to economic analysis, recognizing that global events are filtered through specific national economic structures and policy frameworks, impacting investment patterns and wealth management strategies within the country.

articles.headline: Madras High Court Upholds HR&CE Department's Stance on Temple Land Arrears
articles.explainer:
• The Madras High Court dismissed a writ appeal by Amrutanjan Limited, affirming the Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments (HR&CE) department's claim for ₹9.74 crore in rental arrears for Mylapore Kapaleeswarar Temple land.
• Amrutanjan Limited occupied 14 grounds of temple land on Luz Church Road until 2018, initially under a 99-year lease granted in 1901 to P.R. Sundera Iyer, which expired in 2000.
• The company continued occupation post-lease expiry, paying the original ₹1,400 monthly rent, despite a committee in 2005 fixing the fair rent at ₹3.3 lakh per month from 2001.
• The company had challenged Section 34A(5) of the Tamil Nadu HR&CE Act of 1959, which mandates depositing claimed rental amounts for statutory appeals, but its writ petition was dismissed in 2025.
• The High Court found no evidence that Amrutanjan had filed the statutory appeal or that the HR&CE Commissioner had insisted on the deposit as a pre-condition, thus dismissing the appeal.

This Madras High Court ruling underscores critical aspects of governance concerning religious endowments and public property management in India. The case highlights the challenges faced by statutory bodies like the HR&CE department in recovering dues and enforcing legal provisions, particularly when dealing with long-standing property disputes. The judicial affirmation of the department's powers under the Tamil Nadu HR&CE Act of 1959 reinforces the principle of accountability in managing public trusts and ensuring the financial integrity of religious institutions. This judgment has implications for land administration, revenue recovery mechanisms, and the broader framework of constitutionalism, emphasizing the judiciary's role in upholding the rule of law and protecting institutional assets. It also brings to light the need for robust legal and administrative mechanisms to prevent unauthorized occupation and ensure fair market value for assets belonging to public trusts, contributing to good governance practices.

articles.headline: MP High Court to inspect Bhojshala-Kamal Maula site amid ASI survey review
articles.explainer:
• A Division Bench of the Madhya Pradesh High Court will visit the Bhojshala-Kamal Maula mosque site in Dhar before its next hearing on April 2, 2026.
• The court is hearing petitions related to a survey report submitted by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in July 2024.
• In January 2026, the court allowed all parties to submit objections or suggestions on the ASI report.
• Under a 2003 agreement with the ASI, Hindus perform puja at the complex every Tuesday, and Muslims offer namaz every Friday.
• The Hindu community believes the site is a temple dedicated to Goddess Vagdevi (Saraswati), built by King Bhoj of the Paramara dynasty.

This judicial intervention underscores the complex interplay between historical preservation, religious rights, and the role of the judiciary in resolving sensitive cultural heritage disputes. The High Court's decision to conduct an on-site inspection highlights the principle of judicial review and the meticulous approach required in matters involving archaeological findings and competing claims over places of worship. Such cases often test the secular fabric of the nation and the state's commitment to protecting both religious freedom and historical monuments. The involvement of the Archaeological Survey of India, a statutory body, emphasizes the importance of scientific assessment in resolving such long-standing issues, while also navigating the delicate balance between faith and historical evidence. The outcome could have implications for similar disputes across the country, influencing jurisprudence on religious sites and the interpretation of cultural heritage laws.

articles.headline: India's FDI policy adjustments and China's new Five-Year Plan discussed in weekly review
articles.explainer:
• The weekly review highlighted potential changes or discussions regarding India's Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policies.
• China's new Five-Year Plan (FYP) was also a key point of discussion, outlining its economic and strategic objectives.
• The Five-Year Plan is a series of social and economic development initiatives issued by the Chinese government since 1953.
• India's FDI policy framework is regularly reviewed and updated to attract foreign investment and align with national economic goals.
• The review also touched upon the possible delay in former US President Trump's visit to China, indicating shifts in international diplomatic engagements.

The discussion around India's Foreign Direct Investment policy adjustments underscores the nation's continuous efforts to enhance its economic competitiveness and integrate further into global supply chains. Such policy refinements are crucial for attracting capital, technology, and expertise, thereby fostering economic growth and employment generation, aligning with the objectives of inclusive development. Concurrently, China's new Five-Year Plan provides critical insights into its future economic trajectory, technological advancements, and geopolitical ambitions. Understanding these plans is vital for India's strategic foreign policy and economic planning, particularly concerning trade relations, regional stability, and global governance. The interplay of these domestic and international economic strategies highlights the complex dynamics of global economics and the imperative for nations to adapt their policies to evolving geopolitical landscapes and achieve sustainable development goals.

articles.headline: UK Minister Clarifies NATO's Non-Involvement in Potential US-Israeli Action Against Iran
articles.explainer:
PART 1 — KEY FACTS:
• A UK Minister has reportedly stated that any potential US-Israeli military strike on Iran would "not be a NATO war."
• This statement clarifies the position of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) regarding a hypothetical military confrontation in the Middle East.
• NATO is a collective defence alliance comprising 32 member states from North America and Europe, operating under Article 5 of its founding treaty.
• Article 5 stipulates that an attack against one member is considered an attack against all, triggering collective self-defence.
• The UK is a founding member of NATO, and its minister's statement seeks to delineate the alliance's scope of engagement.
• The context is heightened geopolitical tensions in the Middle East involving Iran, the United States, and Israel.

PART 2 — ANALYSIS: The UK Minister's assertion regarding NATO's non-involvement in a potential US-Israeli strike on Iran highlights the complex dynamics of international alliances and collective security frameworks. While the US is a pivotal NATO member, its bilateral actions, particularly outside the Euro-Atlantic area, do not automatically invoke the alliance's collective defence clause. This distinction is crucial for understanding the limits of multilateral commitments and the sovereignty of member states in their foreign policy decisions. Such statements aim to manage regional escalations and prevent the broadening of conflicts, underscoring the principles of non-proliferation and de-escalation in a volatile geopolitical landscape. For India, these developments in West Asia have implications for energy security, trade routes, and the safety of its diaspora, necessitating a nuanced diplomatic approach.

articles.headline: Centre's Privatisation Policies for PSUs Draw Criticism from Trade Unions
articles.explainer:
• The Centre of Indian Trade Unions (CITU) has criticized the Central government's alleged policies aimed at privatizing the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant (VSP).
• CITU leaders, including state general secretary Ch. Narasing Rao, claimed the Centre intends to weaken VSP to facilitate its handover to private entities.
• They alleged that Central government policies led to VSP incurring losses of ₹5,000 crore last year, despite being profitable and a top exporter until 2021.
• CITU stated that wage revisions, bonuses, and incentives for VSP workers have been halted for five years, forcing permanent and contract workers to accept half salaries.
• The union demanded an end to these practices and the implementation of labor rights, highlighting workers' efforts to save the plant.

The ongoing debate surrounding the privatization of public sector undertakings like the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant underscores critical aspects of India's economic policy and industrial relations. The government's strategic disinvestment policy aims to enhance efficiency and reduce fiscal burden, but it often faces resistance from trade unions concerned about job security, labor rights, and the potential impact on public assets. This situation highlights the tension between economic liberalization and social welfare, a recurring theme in development economics. The allegations of deliberate weakening of a PSU raise questions about governance, transparency, and the long-term vision for India's industrial base. Ensuring a balanced approach that considers both economic viability and the socio-economic implications for workers and regional development is crucial for inclusive growth and maintaining industrial harmony.

articles.headline: ED Arrests Food Company Owner in Money Laundering, Adulterated Dairy Export Case
articles.explainer:
• Enforcement Directorate (ED) arrested Kishan Modi, owner of M/s Jayshri Gayatri Food Products Pvt. Ltd. (JGFPPL), on March 13 under Section 19(1) of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA), 2002.
• The arrest is linked to two money laundering cases lodged by Bhopal police (Aug 2023) and Economic Offences Wing (July 2024).
• JGFPPL, operating under the 'Milk Magic' brand, allegedly manufactured adulterated dairy products by substituting milk fat with palm oil and harmful chemicals.
• These adulterated products were supplied domestically and exported to various countries.
• Forged laboratory test reports were submitted to the Export Inspection Agency, Indore, to obtain export clearances.
• The company received ₹20.59 crore from the export of these adulterated products.

This case underscores critical challenges in regulatory oversight and consumer protection within India's food industry and export sector. The involvement of the Enforcement Directorate highlights the serious economic implications of such malpractices, extending beyond public health concerns to issues of financial integrity and illicit wealth generation under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act. The alleged submission of forged reports to the Export Inspection Agency raises questions about the robustness of quality control mechanisms for exported goods and the potential damage to India's reputation in international trade. Such incidents necessitate a comprehensive review of food safety standards, supply chain transparency, and the enforcement capabilities of regulatory bodies to safeguard public health, ensure fair trade practices, and uphold the principles of good governance. Addressing these systemic vulnerabilities is crucial for fostering inclusive economic growth and maintaining consumer trust.

GS1.headline: Gujarat Court Convicts Five in 2016 Una Dalit Flogging Case
GS1.explainer:
• A court in Veraval, Gujarat, convicted five persons on March 16, 2026, in the 2016 Una Dalit flogging case.
• The incident on July 11, 2016, involved an attack by over 40 men from the Darbar community on four members of the Sarvaiya family in Mota Samadhiyala village near Una.
• The convicted individuals are Ramesh Jadav, Rakesh Joshi, Pramodgiri Gausvami, Nagji Vaniya, and Balwantgiri Gausvami.
• They were found guilty under IPC Sections 323, 324, 342, and 504, pertaining to causing hurt, wrongful confinement, and intentional insult.
• Crucially, convictions were also made under Sections 3(1)(D) and 3(1)(E)(R)(S)(U) of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act.
• 35 other accused were acquitted, and proceedings against one police constable ended due to his death during the trial.

The conviction in the 2016 Una Dalit flogging case, nearly a decade after the incident, underscores the persistent challenges of caste-based discrimination and violence in India. This judgment, particularly the application of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, reinforces the state's commitment to upholding social justice and protecting vulnerable communities. While the legal process has been protracted, the verdict sends a crucial message about accountability and the rule of law in addressing atrocities. It highlights the need for effective implementation of protective legislation, strengthening the criminal justice system, and fostering a societal environment free from caste prejudice. The case also brings to the fore the broader discourse on fundamental rights, equality, and the constitutional mandate to eradicate untouchability, emphasizing the ongoing struggle for dignity and human rights for marginalized sections of society.

GS2.headline: EAM Jaishankar Engages EU Counterparts on West Asia, Ukraine, Trade in Brussels
GS2.explainer:
• External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar visited Brussels for discussions with European Union officials.
• He met with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and was invited to the EU Foreign Affairs Council meeting on March 16, 2026.
• Key topics included the situation in West Asia and Ukraine, with shared objectives of de-escalation, stability, and energy security.
• Discussions also covered trade, investment, technology, mobility, and defence, aiming for early implementation of the EU-India trade deal signed in January.
• The visit underscores India's active engagement with multilateral blocs on critical global and bilateral issues.

The External Affairs Minister's engagement with the European Union leadership and foreign ministers highlights India's proactive diplomacy in navigating complex global challenges and strengthening strategic partnerships. Discussions on West Asia and Ukraine underscore India's commitment to global peace and stability, while also addressing its energy security concerns. The focus on implementing the trade deal reflects India's economic diplomacy, aiming to enhance trade, investment, and technological cooperation, crucial for achieving inclusive growth and sustainable development. This interaction reinforces India's role as a responsible global actor, contributing to a multipolar world order and fostering cooperative federalism in international relations.

GS2.headline: Transgender Communities Nationwide Protest Bill Redefining Gender Identity
GS2.explainer:
• Transgender communities and civil society groups across India are protesting a Union government's proposed legislation.
• The Bill aims to withdraw the right to "self-perceived gender identity" and redefine who constitutes a "transgender person."
• Protests and public meetings have occurred in major cities including New Delhi, Pune, Hyderabad, and Kolkata.
• Coalitions of lawyers, doctors, activists, and professionals are planning campaigns, including writing to MPs and organizing awareness meetings.
• An online public petition demanding the Bill's withdrawal has garnered over 13,000 signatures.
• The protests call for the government to protect the right to self-perceived gender identity, a right previously affirmed by the Supreme Court.

The nationwide protests against the proposed legislation to redefine transgender identity highlight a significant challenge to fundamental rights and social justice in India. The right to self-perceived gender identity is a cornerstone of individual dignity and autonomy, affirmed by the Supreme Court in landmark judgments like NALSA v. Union of India, which recognized transgender persons as the 'third gender' and upheld their rights under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution. Any legislative attempt to restrict this right could be seen as regressive, undermining the principles of equality and non-discrimination. This issue also touches upon the role of civil society in democratic governance, as various groups mobilize to advocate for marginalized communities. Ensuring inclusive growth and protecting the rights of all citizens, including transgender persons, is vital for upholding constitutionalism and fostering a truly equitable society.

GS2.headline: Israel Seeks Diplomatic Resolution with Iran Amidst West Asian Conflict
GS2.explainer:
• Israel's Ambassador to India, Reuven Azar, stated that Israel is not seeking a "perpetual war" with Iran and is open to diplomatic solutions if Iran "changes course."
• The Ambassador's comments came as the West Asia conflict entered its third week, following joint U.S.-Israel strikes on February 28.
• India's External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar confirmed that his talks with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi yielded results for the safe passage of two LNG-laden ships through the Hormuz Strait.
• Jaishankar emphasized India's preference for reasoning and coordination to find solutions in the region, suggesting a similar approach for global stability.
• Israel claimed to support diplomacy and was in contact with regional countries, proposing that a diplomatic way out could follow the completion of military targets.
• The Iranian government has reportedly rejected current talks or a ceasefire, accusing the U.S. of disrupting dialogue through its attacks.

The ongoing West Asian conflict and the diplomatic overtures from Israel, alongside India's active engagement, underscore the complex dynamics of international relations and regional security. India's emphasis on dialogue and its successful mediation for maritime passage through the Strait of Hormuz highlight its growing role as a responsible global actor and a proponent of multilateralism. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical chokepoint for global energy trade, and any disruption has significant implications for energy security and global supply chains, directly affecting India's economic interests. This situation necessitates careful diplomatic maneuvering to de-escalate tensions and protect international maritime law. India's independent foreign policy, balancing relations with all parties, is crucial for maintaining regional stability and safeguarding its strategic autonomy amidst geopolitical shifts.

GS2.headline: India's West Asia Diplomacy Tested Amidst Regional Conflict and Economic Stakes
GS2.explainer:
• The ongoing West Asia conflict, which began on February 28, has extended its impact to South Asia, affecting daily necessities, trade, and the safety of citizens.
• Approximately 25 million South Asians, including 10 million Indians, reside and work in West Asia, making their safety a critical concern.
• Indian seafarers constitute about 15% of the global merchant ship crew, facing heightened risks, particularly near the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint.
• India's initial diplomatic posture regarding the conflict, specifically its delayed condolence for Iran's Supreme Leader, contrasted with immediate responses from neighbouring South Asian nations like Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Maldives.
• The conflict has disrupted supply chains for food, fuel, and fertilizers, impacting economic stability across the South Asian region.

This article highlights the complex challenges confronting India's foreign policy and neighbourhood diplomacy in the volatile West Asian region. The conflict's spillover effects, particularly on the large Indian diaspora and maritime trade routes like the Strait of Hormuz, underscore the intricate linkages between regional stability and India's economic interests and national security. India's strategic autonomy and its ability to maintain balanced relations with all stakeholders, including the U.S., Israel, and Iran, are being rigorously tested. The differing diplomatic responses among South Asian nations also reveal the nuances of regional cooperation and the potential for divergent foreign policy approaches. This situation necessitates a robust diplomatic framework to safeguard national interests, ensure the welfare of overseas citizens, and contribute to regional de-escalation, aligning with India's broader vision of a multipolar world order and responsible global citizenship.

GS2.headline: China-US Engagements Continue Amidst Sanctions Discussions
GS2.explainer:
• China has confirmed ongoing contact with the United States regarding a potential summit between the two nations.
• Beijing has also indicated that sanctions imposed by U.S. Senator Marco Rubio may not apply, suggesting a nuanced diplomatic approach.
• These developments occur amidst complex bilateral relations, marked by trade disputes, technological competition, and geopolitical rivalries.
• High-level dialogues between the U.S. and China are critical for global stability and addressing shared challenges like climate change and economic recovery.
• The potential for a summit and the discussion around sanctions reflect efforts to manage tensions and find areas of cooperation between the two global powers.

The continued engagement between China and the United States, despite prevailing geopolitical tensions and the shadow of sanctions, underscores the intricate nature of great power competition and cooperation. For India, the dynamics of US-China relations hold significant implications for its strategic autonomy, economic partnerships, and regional security architecture. A stable yet competitive relationship between these two powers can create opportunities for India to advance its own interests, while heightened friction could necessitate careful diplomatic balancing. This ongoing dialogue highlights the imperative for multilateralism and a rules-based international order, as the policies of these major economies inevitably shape global trade, technology governance, and the broader international relations landscape, influencing India's foreign policy calculus and economic development trajectory.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Calcutta High Court's NIA Probe Order in Beldanga Violence
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court declined to intervene in a Calcutta High Court decision that refused to stay a National Investigation Agency (NIA) probe into violence in Beldanga, Murshidabad district, West Bengal.
• The Calcutta High Court had confirmed a trial court order to hand over the case diary to the NIA and directed the agency to file a status report by March 24.
• West Bengal challenged the High Court's direction, arguing that the Supreme Court had previously questioned the invocation of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (UAPA) in the case.
• On February 11, the apex court had expressed doubts whether an "emotional outburst" could be termed a "terrorist act" under UAPA without material evidence.
• The Supreme Court, however, found the High Court's confirmation of the NIA probe "quite balanced," stating it was for the High Court to impartially decide on the necessity of an NIA investigation.

This Supreme Court ruling reinforces the principles of judicial review and federalism, particularly concerning the jurisdiction of central investigative agencies and the application of anti-terror legislation. The apex court's decision to uphold the Calcutta High Court's order, despite its earlier reservations on the UAPA's invocation, highlights the judiciary's role in ensuring a balanced approach to law enforcement and constitutional governance. It underscores the delicate balance between state autonomy in maintaining law and order and the central government's prerogative in matters of national security, especially when scheduled offences under acts like UAPA are involved. This case is crucial for understanding the operational dynamics between the High Courts and the Supreme Court, the scope of the NIA Act, and the interpretation of "terrorist act" under UAPA, which has significant implications for fundamental rights and criminal justice administration.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court expands Right to Die with Dignity under Article 21, approves withdrawal of life support
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark decision, approved the withdrawal of life support, including artificial nutrition and hydration, for Harish Rana, who was in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for over 12 years.
• The Court invoked the constitutional idea of dignity, stating it's incompatible to keep terminally ill patients in PVS alive solely through technological advancements.
• This judgment expands the right to live with dignity under Article 21 of the Constitution to encompass the right to die with dignity.
• The ruling builds upon previous cases like Smt. Gian Kaur vs The State Of Punjab (1996) and the Aruna Shanbaug petition (2011), where guidelines for withdrawing medical treatment were laid down.
• A 2018 Supreme Court Constitution Bench had already held that the right to die with dignity is a fundamental right.
• The Court also endorsed the concept of a 'living will', a legal document allowing individuals to state preferences for medical treatment if incapacitated.

This Supreme Court judgment significantly reinforces the constitutional right to life and personal liberty under Article 21, interpreting it to include the right to die with dignity. By allowing the withdrawal of life support for individuals in a persistent vegetative state, the judiciary has provided a compassionate framework for end-of-life care, addressing the ethical dilemmas posed by advanced medical technology. This ruling reflects a progressive stance on individual autonomy and human dignity, ensuring that prolonged suffering without hope of recovery does not diminish a person's fundamental rights. The endorsement of 'living wills' empowers citizens to make informed decisions about their medical treatment, promoting patient-centric healthcare and reducing the burden on families. This judicial pronouncement, in the absence of specific legislation, underscores the Supreme Court's role as a guardian of fundamental rights and its capacity to evolve jurisprudence in response to societal and technological changes, impacting public policy on healthcare and individual liberties.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Emphasizes Stakeholder Consultation for Legal Education Reforms
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India expressed reluctance to alter the duration of five-year integrated LL.B. programmes across the country.
• A Bench comprising Chief Justice of India Surya Kant and Justice Joymalya Bagchi stated that policy matters like course duration do not warrant judicial intervention.
• The Court acknowledged the necessity of reforms to strengthen the quality of legal education in India.
• It observed that decisions concerning the structure and duration of professional courses require broader consultation.
• Key stakeholders for such consultations include academic institutions, regulatory bodies (like the Bar Council of India), and other relevant parties.

The Supreme Court's stance on the duration of legal education programs highlights the principle of separation of powers, emphasizing that policy formulation primarily rests with the executive and legislative branches, guided by expert bodies. While the judiciary often intervenes to uphold constitutional rights or ensure good governance, it generally refrains from encroaching upon areas requiring specialized academic and administrative expertise. The Court's call for extensive stakeholder consultation underscores the importance of inclusive decision-making in reforming professional education, ensuring that changes are well-considered, practical, and reflect the diverse needs of students, practitioners, and the legal system. This approach promotes cooperative federalism in education policy and strengthens institutional autonomy, crucial for maintaining the quality and relevance of legal professionals in a dynamic society.

GS2.headline: MEA Rejects USCIRF Report, Cites Distorted India Picture and Temple Vandalism in U.S.
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) criticized the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF) for its latest report on India.
• MEA spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal stated that USCIRF presents a "distorted and selective picture of India," relying on "questionable sources and ideological narratives."
• The MEA urged USCIRF to highlight incidents of vandalism targeting Hindu temples in the United States instead of criticizing India.
• The USCIRF report had called for "targeted sanctions" against India's Research & Analysis Wing (R&AW) and the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS).
• These sanctions proposed freezing assets and barring entry into the U.S. for individuals and entities deemed responsible for religious freedom violations.

The MEA's strong rebuttal to the USCIRF report underscores India's firm stance against external interference in its internal affairs and its commitment to protecting its national sovereignty. The criticism of the report's methodology highlights concerns about objective reporting and the potential for biased narratives to influence international perceptions. This diplomatic exchange reflects the complexities of bilateral relations, particularly when issues of human rights and religious freedom are raised by international bodies. India's counter-narrative, by pointing to incidents affecting its diaspora abroad, emphasizes the principle of reciprocity and the need for a balanced perspective in assessing religious freedom globally, aligning with its foreign policy objectives of non-interference and mutual respect.

GS2.headline: Madras High Court Upholds HR&CE Department's Stance on Temple Land Arrears
GS2.explainer:
• The Madras High Court dismissed a writ appeal by Amrutanjan Limited, affirming the Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments (HR&CE) department's claim for ₹9.74 crore in rental arrears for Mylapore Kapaleeswarar Temple land.
• Amrutanjan Limited occupied 14 grounds of temple land on Luz Church Road until 2018, initially under a 99-year lease granted in 1901 to P.R. Sundera Iyer, which expired in 2000.
• The company continued occupation post-lease expiry, paying the original ₹1,400 monthly rent, despite a committee in 2005 fixing the fair rent at ₹3.3 lakh per month from 2001.
• The company had challenged Section 34A(5) of the Tamil Nadu HR&CE Act of 1959, which mandates depositing claimed rental amounts for statutory appeals, but its writ petition was dismissed in 2025.
• The High Court found no evidence that Amrutanjan had filed the statutory appeal or that the HR&CE Commissioner had insisted on the deposit as a pre-condition, thus dismissing the appeal.

This Madras High Court ruling underscores critical aspects of governance concerning religious endowments and public property management in India. The case highlights the challenges faced by statutory bodies like the HR&CE department in recovering dues and enforcing legal provisions, particularly when dealing with long-standing property disputes. The judicial affirmation of the department's powers under the Tamil Nadu HR&CE Act of 1959 reinforces the principle of accountability in managing public trusts and ensuring the financial integrity of religious institutions. This judgment has implications for land administration, revenue recovery mechanisms, and the broader framework of constitutionalism, emphasizing the judiciary's role in upholding the rule of law and protecting institutional assets. It also brings to light the need for robust legal and administrative mechanisms to prevent unauthorized occupation and ensure fair market value for assets belonging to public trusts, contributing to good governance practices.

GS2.headline: UK Minister Clarifies NATO's Non-Involvement in Potential US-Israeli Action Against Iran
GS2.explainer:
PART 1 — KEY FACTS:
• A UK Minister has reportedly stated that any potential US-Israeli military strike on Iran would "not be a NATO war."
• This statement clarifies the position of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) regarding a hypothetical military confrontation in the Middle East.
• NATO is a collective defence alliance comprising 32 member states from North America and Europe, operating under Article 5 of its founding treaty.
• Article 5 stipulates that an attack against one member is considered an attack against all, triggering collective self-defence.
• The UK is a founding member of NATO, and its minister's statement seeks to delineate the alliance's scope of engagement.
• The context is heightened geopolitical tensions in the Middle East involving Iran, the United States, and Israel.

PART 2 — ANALYSIS: The UK Minister's assertion regarding NATO's non-involvement in a potential US-Israeli strike on Iran highlights the complex dynamics of international alliances and collective security frameworks. While the US is a pivotal NATO member, its bilateral actions, particularly outside the Euro-Atlantic area, do not automatically invoke the alliance's collective defence clause. This distinction is crucial for understanding the limits of multilateral commitments and the sovereignty of member states in their foreign policy decisions. Such statements aim to manage regional escalations and prevent the broadening of conflicts, underscoring the principles of non-proliferation and de-escalation in a volatile geopolitical landscape. For India, these developments in West Asia have implications for energy security, trade routes, and the safety of its diaspora, necessitating a nuanced diplomatic approach.

GS3.headline: National Quantum Mission Advances: 23 Institutions Approved for Quantum Labs
GS3.explainer:
• Twenty-three academic institutions across India have received approval to establish quantum teaching laboratories.
• This initiative falls under the ambit of the National Quantum Mission (NQM), a flagship government program.
• The NQM has an approved budget of ₹6003.65 crore for the period 2023–2031.
• Its ambitious goals include developing 50–1,000 qubit quantum computers and satellite-based secure communication.
• The mission also aims to advance high-precision quantum sensors and materials, indicating a comprehensive approach to quantum technology.

The approval of quantum teaching laboratories under the National Quantum Mission marks a significant stride in India's pursuit of cutting-edge scientific and technological advancements. This strategic investment in quantum computing, communication, and sensing is crucial for national security, economic competitiveness, and future innovation. By fostering research and development in academic institutions, the government aims to build a robust ecosystem for quantum technologies, aligning with the vision of Atmanirbhar Bharat. This initiative will contribute to human resource development in specialized fields, address the digital divide by enabling secure communication, and potentially revolutionize sectors from healthcare to defense, thereby promoting sustainable development and technological sovereignty.

GS3.headline: Geopolitical Risks Drive Oil Price Volatility, Impacting Global Energy Security
GS3.explainer:
• Crude oil prices surged to $118 a barrel last week, reflecting heightened geopolitical tensions, particularly in West Asia.
• Prices have more than doubled since mid-December 2025, when Brent crude was at $57.56, and remain in three digits.
• The ongoing conflict involving Israel and Iran has created tangible barriers to supply, effectively sidelining 20% of global supplies.
• Vulnerability of global maritime arteries like the Strait of Hormuz (one-fifth of global oil consumption) and Bab el-Mandeb/Suez corridor (one-tenth of seaborne crude) is stark.
• Geopolitical events now directly influence freight rates, shipping insurance, and vessel availability, transforming oil into a political instrument.

The analysis highlights a critical shift in global energy markets, where geopolitical risks now exert a profound and sustained influence on crude oil prices beyond traditional supply-demand dynamics. For major importing economies like India, this volatility poses significant challenges to energy security, fiscal stability, and economic development. The vulnerability of strategic maritime chokepoints underscores the imperative for diversified energy sources, robust trade strategies, and proactive diplomatic engagement to mitigate supply chain disruptions. This situation necessitates a comprehensive approach to foreign policy and economic planning, emphasizing strategic autonomy and resilience against external shocks, while also impacting inflation and the balance of payments, crucial for macroeconomic stability.

GS3.headline: Western Himalayas Experience Driest Spring in Four Centuries, Study Reveals
GS3.explainer:
• Researchers reconstructed 396 years of spring rainfall history in the Western Himalayas, specifically Lahaul, Himachal Pradesh, using tree cores from old deodar trees.
• The study, published by scientists from Birbal Sahni Institute of Palaeosciences (BSIP) and other institutions, identified the spring of 2022 as the driest on record.
• The last two decades (2002-2022) have been identified as the driest 20-year period in the entire 396-year reconstruction.
• Dendroclimatology, the science of determining past climates from tree rings, was employed, where tree ring width indicates past water availability.
• The findings have significant implications for water security, disaster management, and agriculture in northern India, which heavily relies on Himalayan snowmelt.
• Permission from the Forest Department was obtained to extract tree core samples using an increment borer, ensuring minimal damage to the trees.

This research underscores the critical impact of climate change on India's ecological balance and water resources, particularly in the sensitive Himalayan region. The unprecedented dryness in the Western Himalayas, as revealed by dendroclimatology, highlights a severe environmental challenge with far-reaching socio-economic consequences. Diminished snowmelt directly threatens the water security of downstream regions, impacting agricultural productivity and exacerbating the vulnerability to natural disasters like droughts and flash floods. This situation necessitates robust climate adaptation strategies, integrated water resource management, and enhanced disaster preparedness. The findings reinforce the urgency of national climate action plans and international cooperation to mitigate global warming, aligning with India's commitments under the Paris Agreement and Sustainable Development Goals related to climate resilience and water management. Understanding these long-term climate shifts is crucial for informed policy-making in environmental governance and sustainable development.

GS3.headline: NavIC Satellite Faces Setback with Atomic Clock Failure, Impacts India's Navigation Autonomy
GS3.explainer:
PART 1 — KEY FACTS:
• India's indigenous navigation satellite system, NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation), has reportedly experienced a failure in one of its onboard atomic clocks.
• Atomic clocks are crucial for precise timing and accurate positional data, which are fundamental to the functioning of satellite navigation systems.
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) operates NavIC, which currently comprises a constellation of seven satellites.
• This incident could potentially affect the accuracy and reliability of the specific satellite's signals, impacting the overall system's performance.
• NavIC aims to provide accurate real-time positioning and timing services over India and a region extending up to 1,500 km around its boundary.
• The system is designed to provide both Standard Positioning Service (SPS) for civilian users and Restricted Service (RS) for authorised users, including the military.

PART 2 — ANALYSIS: The reported failure of an atomic clock on a NavIC satellite underscores the technical complexities inherent in developing and maintaining advanced space infrastructure. This incident has significant implications for India's strategic autonomy, as NavIC is envisioned to reduce reliance on foreign global navigation satellite systems like GPS. The precision offered by atomic clocks is vital for applications ranging from disaster management and fleet tracking to critical infrastructure and defence. Ensuring the resilience and redundancy of such critical components is paramount for national security and economic development. This event highlights the ongoing challenges in indigenous technology development and the need for robust quality control and backup mechanisms to sustain critical space-based assets, aligning with India's broader vision for self-reliance in space technology.

GS3.headline: Indian Navy Deploys Warships to Escort Merchant Vessels in Gulf Region
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Navy has deployed warships to escort Indian-flagged merchant vessels transiting through the Persian Gulf region.
• Three Indian Navy ships (Shivalik, Nanda Devi, and Jag Laadki) escorted merchant ships from the Gulf of Oman after they crossed the Strait of Hormuz.
• This deployment ensures the safe transit of Indian vessels through vulnerable international waters.
• The warships are operating under "Operation Sankalp," a mission specifically aimed at safeguarding merchant shipping and protecting India’s maritime interests.
• The mission focuses on the Gulf region, which is crucial for India's trade and energy security.
• The deployment comes amidst heightened regional tensions, emphasizing the need for robust maritime security.

The Indian Navy's deployment of warships to escort merchant vessels in the Gulf of Oman and through the Strait of Hormuz underscores India's commitment to protecting its maritime interests and ensuring the security of global trade routes. "Operation Sankalp" exemplifies India's growing blue-water capabilities and its role as a net security provider in the Indian Ocean Region. This proactive measure is vital for safeguarding India's energy security, as a significant portion of its oil and gas imports transits through these waters. It also reinforces adherence to the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) regarding freedom of navigation. Such deployments are crucial for maintaining regional stability, deterring piracy and other maritime threats, and projecting India's strategic autonomy and influence in a geopolitically sensitive area.

Security: Role of External State and Non-state Actors in creating challenges to internal security
Security: Border Areas and their Management

GS3.headline: Analysis of Subdued Gold Prices in India Amidst West Asian Conflict
GS3.explainer:
• Despite the ongoing West Asian conflict, which typically drives up safe-haven asset demand, gold prices in India have remained subdued.
• This phenomenon contrasts with global trends where geopolitical instability often correlates with rising gold valuations.
• Factors influencing domestic gold prices include local supply-demand dynamics, import duties, exchange rate fluctuations, and investor sentiment.
• The Indian market's unique characteristics, such as seasonal demand patterns and cultural significance of gold, play a role in its pricing.
• The Reserve Bank of India's monetary policy decisions and government's fiscal measures can also indirectly impact gold's attractiveness as an investment.

The divergence of India's gold market from global trends amidst geopolitical tensions in West Asia presents an interesting case study in economic resilience and domestic market dynamics. While gold traditionally serves as a hedge against inflation and uncertainty, its subdued performance in India suggests the overriding influence of internal economic factors. This scenario highlights the complex interplay of fiscal policy, monetary policy, and consumer behaviour in shaping commodity markets. Understanding these dynamics is crucial for macroeconomic stability, as gold imports significantly impact India's current account deficit. Furthermore, it underscores the need for a nuanced approach to economic analysis, recognizing that global events are filtered through specific national economic structures and policy frameworks, impacting investment patterns and wealth management strategies within the country.

Analyze the macroeconomic implications of gold demand and imports for the Indian economy.

GS3.headline: India's FDI policy adjustments and China's new Five-Year Plan discussed in weekly review
GS3.explainer:
• The weekly review highlighted potential changes or discussions regarding India's Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policies.
• China's new Five-Year Plan (FYP) was also a key point of discussion, outlining its economic and strategic objectives.
• The Five-Year Plan is a series of social and economic development initiatives issued by the Chinese government since 1953.
• India's FDI policy framework is regularly reviewed and updated to attract foreign investment and align with national economic goals.
• The review also touched upon the possible delay in former US President Trump's visit to China, indicating shifts in international diplomatic engagements.

The discussion around India's Foreign Direct Investment policy adjustments underscores the nation's continuous efforts to enhance its economic competitiveness and integrate further into global supply chains. Such policy refinements are crucial for attracting capital, technology, and expertise, thereby fostering economic growth and employment generation, aligning with the objectives of inclusive development. Concurrently, China's new Five-Year Plan provides critical insights into its future economic trajectory, technological advancements, and geopolitical ambitions. Understanding these plans is vital for India's strategic foreign policy and economic planning, particularly concerning trade relations, regional stability, and global governance. The interplay of these domestic and international economic strategies highlights the complex dynamics of global economics and the imperative for nations to adapt their policies to evolving geopolitical landscapes and achieve sustainable development goals.

GS3.headline: Centre's Privatisation Policies for PSUs Draw Criticism from Trade Unions
GS3.explainer:
• The Centre of Indian Trade Unions (CITU) has criticized the Central government's alleged policies aimed at privatizing the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant (VSP).
• CITU leaders, including state general secretary Ch. Narasing Rao, claimed the Centre intends to weaken VSP to facilitate its handover to private entities.
• They alleged that Central government policies led to VSP incurring losses of ₹5,000 crore last year, despite being profitable and a top exporter until 2021.
• CITU stated that wage revisions, bonuses, and incentives for VSP workers have been halted for five years, forcing permanent and contract workers to accept half salaries.
• The union demanded an end to these practices and the implementation of labor rights, highlighting workers' efforts to save the plant.

The ongoing debate surrounding the privatization of public sector undertakings like the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant underscores critical aspects of India's economic policy and industrial relations. The government's strategic disinvestment policy aims to enhance efficiency and reduce fiscal burden, but it often faces resistance from trade unions concerned about job security, labor rights, and the potential impact on public assets. This situation highlights the tension between economic liberalization and social welfare, a recurring theme in development economics. The allegations of deliberate weakening of a PSU raise questions about governance, transparency, and the long-term vision for India's industrial base. Ensuring a balanced approach that considers both economic viability and the socio-economic implications for workers and regional development is crucial for inclusive growth and maintaining industrial harmony.

GS3.headline: ED Arrests Food Company Owner in Money Laundering, Adulterated Dairy Export Case
GS3.explainer:
• Enforcement Directorate (ED) arrested Kishan Modi, owner of M/s Jayshri Gayatri Food Products Pvt. Ltd. (JGFPPL), on March 13 under Section 19(1) of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA), 2002.
• The arrest is linked to two money laundering cases lodged by Bhopal police (Aug 2023) and Economic Offences Wing (July 2024).
• JGFPPL, operating under the 'Milk Magic' brand, allegedly manufactured adulterated dairy products by substituting milk fat with palm oil and harmful chemicals.
• These adulterated products were supplied domestically and exported to various countries.
• Forged laboratory test reports were submitted to the Export Inspection Agency, Indore, to obtain export clearances.
• The company received ₹20.59 crore from the export of these adulterated products.

This case underscores critical challenges in regulatory oversight and consumer protection within India's food industry and export sector. The involvement of the Enforcement Directorate highlights the serious economic implications of such malpractices, extending beyond public health concerns to issues of financial integrity and illicit wealth generation under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act. The alleged submission of forged reports to the Export Inspection Agency raises questions about the robustness of quality control mechanisms for exported goods and the potential damage to India's reputation in international trade. Such incidents necessitate a comprehensive review of food safety standards, supply chain transparency, and the enforcement capabilities of regulatory bodies to safeguard public health, ensure fair trade practices, and uphold the principles of good governance. Addressing these systemic vulnerabilities is crucial for fostering inclusive economic growth and maintaining consumer trust.
